figures.caption: Figure 1. Majority-class bias. TabPFN suffers from majorityclass bias, resulting in poor recall for the minority class.
Figure 2. Direct utilization of train-set in TabPFN-based models.
Figure 3. Robustness to shift with DistPFN.
Table 3. vs. Label shift methods. DistPFN operates in a post-hoc manner without test prior estimation or model retraining.

Table 12. Dataset selection with K-means clustering. Our method remains effective when training subsets are formed by sampling a proportion (P ) from each of the K = 10 clusters, demonstrating robustness to the choice of training subsets.

Figure 4. Overall framework of DistPFN. (a) TabPFN exhibits a majority-class bias under label shift, predicting test instances toward the majority class in the training dataset. (b) DistPFN mitigates this bias via a simple test-time adaptation method that rescales the predicted class probabilities for each test instance.
Table 4. Prediction w/ DistPFN and DistPFN-T.
Table 21. Accuracy improvement by base model accuracy quartile.
Table 19. DistPFN applied to tree-based models.
Figure 5. Inverse-frequency-based oversampling. As β increases, the training distribution becomes increasingly biased toward rare classes, while the test distribution remains fixed
